unit.name: Perplexity for evaluating language models
unit.description: Defines perplexity, connects it to cross-entropy, and uses historical perplexity numbers to show why neural language models mattered.
unit.summary: The lecture finishes the language-model evaluation story by defining perplexity as the standard metric for scoring how well a model predicts held-out human text [ts=199s]. It explicitly links perplexity to the exponential of cross-entropy / per-word negative log-likelihood, while warning that comparisons depend on the log base being used [ts=247s] [ts=275s]. The segment also gives the historical arc from smoothed n-gram models to RNN/LSTM language models: interpolated Kneser-Ney n-grams are described around perplexity 67, early improved neural models around the 40s to 30s, and modern systems in the single digits, emphasizing that lower perplexity means better next-word prediction [ts=441s] [ts=537s].

key_points.text: Perplexity is the standard held-out evaluation measure for language models.
Perplexity is the exponential of cross-entropy / per-word negative log-likelihood.
Perplexity values depend on the logarithm base, so numbers are not comparable unless the base matches.
Interpolated Kneser-Ney n-gram models were around perplexity 67, while LSTM language models pushed much lower and modern models reach single digits.

unit_summary_for_generation: The lecture finishes the language-model evaluation story by defining perplexity as the standard metric for scoring how well a model predicts held-out human text [ts=199s]. It explicitly links perplexity to the exponential of cross-entropy / per-word negative log-likelihood, while warning that comparisons depend on the log base being used [ts=247s] [ts=275s]. The segment also gives the historical arc from smoothed n-gram models to RNN/LSTM language models: interpolated Kneser-Ney n-grams are described around perplexity 67, early improved neural models around the 40s to 30s, and modern systems in the single digits, emphasizing that lower perplexity means better next-word prediction [ts=441s] [ts=537s].

kp_catalog.name: Perplexity as a proxy metric
kp_catalog.description: Pretraining or validation perplexity often tracks downstream performance but is not universally comparable or sufficient.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because perplexity as a proxy metric clarifies pretraining or validation perplexity often tracks downstream performance but is not universally comparable or sufficient, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Progress from n-gram models to neural language models
kp_catalog.description: Historical perplexity comparisons show why LSTMs and later models were seen as major advances over smoothed n-gram language models.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Progress from n-gram models to neural language models captures the core idea that Historical perplexity comparisons show why LSTMs and later models were seen as major advances over smoothed n-gram language models.
